name: anova decomposition in regression
description: A decomposition of the regression function into main effects, two-way interactions, and higher-order interactions: f(X) = α + Σⱼ gⱼ(Xⱼ) + Σⱼ<ₖ gⱼₖ(Xⱼ,Xₖ) + ···, used to introduce structure by eliminating higher-order terms.

name: trellis display
description: A visualization method showing the response as a function of one variable, conditioned on ranges of other variables, implemented as a grid of panels each showing a slice of the data.
section: 6.4 Structured Local Regression Models in IRp

name: structured kernels
description: Kernels using a positive semidefinite matrix A to weight different coordinates: Kλ,A(x₀,x) = D(||(x-x₀)ᵀA||/λ), allowing individual predictors to be downgraded or entire directions to be omitted.

name: instantaneous prefix code
description: A coding scheme where no code is the prefix of any other, allowing the receiver to know exactly when a message has been completely sent without requiring delimiters.

name: huffman coding
description: A practical coding procedure that can get close to the Shannon entropy lower bound for average message length.

name: learning curve
description: A plot of prediction performance (e.g., 1-Err) versus training set size N, showing how classifier performance improves with more data and used to assess cross-validation bias.

name: .632 estimator
description: A bootstrap estimate of prediction error designed to alleviate the upward bias of leave-one-out bootstrap by pulling it toward the training error rate using the constant .632.

name: .632+ estimator
description: An improved version of the .632 estimator that accounts for overfitting by adjusting the weight between leave-one-out bootstrap and training error based on the relative overfitting rate.

name: leave-one-out bootstrap
description: A bootstrap estimation method that only uses predictions from bootstrap samples not containing each observation, avoiding the overlap bias of naive bootstrap.

name: no-information error rate
description: The error rate of a prediction rule if the inputs and class labels were independent, used to assess the amount of overfitting in bootstrap estimates.

name: relative overfitting rate
description: A quantity measuring the degree of overfitting, ranging from 0 (no overfitting) to 1 (overfitting equals the no-information value), used in the .632+ estimator.

name: parametric bootstrap
description: A bootstrap method that simulates new responses by adding Gaussian noise to predicted values, rather than sampling from raw data.

name: likelihood function
description: The probability of the observed data under a parametric model, defined as the product of densities over all observations.

name: score function
description: The derivative of the log-likelihood with respect to parameters, which equals zero at the maximum likelihood estimate.

name: information matrix
description: The negative second derivative matrix of the log-likelihood, used to assess precision of maximum likelihood estimates.

name: observed information
description: The information matrix evaluated at the maximum likelihood estimate.

name: fisher information
description: The expected value of the information matrix, also called expected information.

name: noninformative prior
description: A constant or very diffuse prior distribution that has minimal influence on the posterior, allowing data to dominate inference.

name: predictive distribution
description: The Bayesian distribution for future observations, integrating over the posterior distribution of parameters.

name: gaussian process prior
description: A prior distribution on functions specified by defining the covariance between any two function values.

name: covariance kernel
description: A function defining the covariance structure of a Gaussian process prior.

name: responsibility
description: In the EM algorithm for Gaussian mixtures, the weight assigned to each observation for each cluster based on the likelihood of the corresponding Gaussians.

name: expectation-maximization algorithm
description: An iterative algorithm for maximum likelihood estimation in problems with latent variables, alternating between computing expected values of latent data (E-step) and maximizing likelihood (M-step).

name: latent data
description: Unobserved or missing data that, if known, would simplify likelihood maximization in the EM algorithm.

name: complete data log-likelihood
description: The log-likelihood based on both observed and latent data, used in the EM algorithm framework.

name: generalized em algorithms
description: Variants of EM that only require increasing (not maximizing) the Q function in the M-step.

name: markov chain monte carlo
description: A class of algorithms for sampling from probability distributions by constructing a Markov chain with the desired distribution as its stationary distribution.

name: gibbs sampling
description: An MCMC method that generates samples from a joint distribution by iteratively sampling from conditional distributions of each variable given the others.

name: stationary distribution
description: In MCMC, the limiting distribution of the Markov chain, which is the target distribution from which we wish to sample.

name: burn-in period
description: The initial iterations of MCMC that are discarded before the chain reaches stationarity.

name: bagging
description: Bootstrap aggregation: a method that averages predictions over bootstrap samples to reduce variance and improve prediction accuracy.

name: empirical distribution
description: A distribution putting equal probability on each observed data point, used as the sampling distribution in nonparametric bootstrap.

name: consensus vote
description: In bagging for classification, combining predictions by majority voting across bootstrap samples.

name: weak learners
description: Classifiers with error rate better than random but not necessarily strong, which can be combined to form a strong classifier.

name: bayesian model averaging
description: A Bayesian approach to combining predictions from multiple models, weighting each by its posterior probability.

name: committee methods
description: Model averaging strategies that take a simple unweighted average of predictions from multiple models.

name: stacking
description: Stacked generalization: a model averaging method that uses cross-validated predictions to learn optimal weights for combining models, accounting for model complexity.

name: bumping
description: Bootstrap-based model improvement that fits models to multiple bootstrap samples and selects the one with best training set performance, helping escape poor local minima.

name: generalized additive model
description: A regression model where the response is related to predictors through a sum of smooth nonparametric functions, extending linear models to allow flexible nonlinear effects.

name: additive logistic regression
description: A generalized additive model for binary outcomes using the logit link, replacing linear terms with smooth functions.

name: link function
description: A function relating the conditional mean of a response to an additive function of predictors in generalized models.

name: identity link
description: The link function g(μ) = μ, used for linear and additive models with Gaussian responses.

name: logit link
description: The link function g(μ) = log[μ/(1−μ)] for modeling binomial probabilities.

name: probit link
description: The inverse Gaussian CDF as a link function for modeling binomial probabilities.

name: log link
description: The link function g(μ) = log(μ) for log-linear or log-additive models with Poisson count data.

name: backfitting algorithm
description: An iterative procedure for fitting additive models by cycling through predictors, applying a smoother to partial residuals for each predictor while holding others fixed.

name: local scoring algorithm
description: An iteratively reweighted least squares algorithm for fitting generalized additive models, combining backfitting with Newton-Raphson optimization.

name: recursive binary partition
description: A tree-building strategy that splits feature space into regions by recursively splitting into two subregions at each step, using axis-parallel splits.

name: cost-complexity pruning
description: A tree pruning method that balances tree size against goodness of fit by minimizing a penalized criterion Cα(T) = Σ NmQm(T) + α|T|.

name: weakest link pruning
description: A method to find the optimal subtree Tα by successively collapsing internal nodes that produce the smallest per-node increase in node impurity.

name: node impurity
description: A measure of heterogeneity within a tree node, used to guide splitting decisions. For classification, common measures are Gini index, cross-entropy, and misclassification error.

name: gini index
description: A node impurity measure for classification trees equal to Σk≠k' p̂mk p̂mk' = ΣK p̂mk(1 - p̂mk), measuring the probability of misclassification if class labels are randomly assigned according to node proportions.

name: cross-entropy for trees
description: A node impurity measure for classification trees defined as -ΣK p̂mk log p̂mk, which is more sensitive to changes in node probabilities than misclassification rate.

name: surrogate splits
description: Alternative splitting predictors used in tree construction when the primary splitting predictor has missing values, exploiting correlations between predictors.

name: sensitivity in classification
description: The probability of predicting disease (positive class) given the true state is disease; also called true positive rate.

name: specificity in classification
description: The probability of predicting non-disease (negative class) given the true state is non-disease; also called true negative rate.

name: receiver operating characteristic curve
description: A plot of sensitivity versus specificity as classification threshold varies, used to assess the tradeoff between true positive and false positive rates. Area under ROC curve summarizes classifier performance.

name: c-statistic
description: The area under the ROC curve, equivalent to the Mann-Whitney U statistic for the median difference between prediction scores in two groups.

name: patient rule induction method
description:
2. Consider shrinking the box by compressing one face, so as to peel oﬀ
the proportion α of observations having either the highest values of
a predictor Xj, or the lowest. Choose the peeling that produces the
highest response mean in the remaining box. (Typically α = 0.05 or
0.10.)

name: top-down peeling
description: PRIM's greedy algorithm that starts with a box containing all data, then repeatedly compresses one face to peel off observations, choosing the compression that maximizes the box mean.

name: mars basis functions
description: Piecewise linear functions (x-t)+ and (t-x)+ used in MARS, forming reflected pairs with knots at observed values. Products of these create tensor products for modeling interactions.

name: reflected pair
description: In MARS, the two complementary basis functions (x-t)+ and (t-x)+ that form a knot at value t, allowing locally adaptive modeling.

name: generalized cross-validation for mars
description: A criterion GCV(λ) = Σ(yi - f̂λ(xi))²/N / [1 - M(λ)/N]² used to select model size in MARS, accounting for effective parameters including knot selection penalty.

name: hierarchical mixtures of experts
description: A tree-based method with soft probabilistic splits instead of hard decisions, fitting linear or logistic regression models in terminal nodes (experts) combined by gating networks.

name: gating networks in hme
description: Soft multinomial logistic functions gj(x,γj) = e^(γjᵀx) / Σ e^(γkᵀx) at internal nodes of HME that assign observations to branches probabilistically.

name: missing at random
description: Data are MAR if the distribution of the missingness indicator R depends on the data Z only through observed entries Zobs, not on unobserved values.

name: missing completely at random
description: Data are MCAR if the distribution of missingness doesn't depend on observed or missing data: Pr(R|Z,θ) = Pr(R|θ). Stronger assumption than MAR.

name: adaboost.m1
description: A boosting algorithm that sequentially applies a weak classifier to reweighted data, upweighting misclassified observations, and combines classifiers via weighted majority vote.

name: weak classifier
description: A classifier whose error rate is only slightly better than random guessing. Boosting combines many weak classifiers to produce a powerful committee.

name: forward stagewise additive modeling
description: An approach to fitting additive models f(x) = Σ βm b(x;γm) by sequentially adding basis functions without modifying previously added terms.

name: exponential loss for classification
description: Loss function L(y, f(x)) = exp(-y f(x)) used by AdaBoost, where y ∈ {-1,1}. Leads to simple reweighting algorithm but sensitive to outliers.

name: binomial deviance
description: Loss function -[y' log p(x) + (1-y') log(1-p(x))] for binary classification, where p(x) = e^f(x)/(1+e^f(x)). More robust than exponential loss to outliers and mislabeling.

name: multinomial deviance
description: Extension of binomial deviance to K classes: -Σ I(yi=Gk) log pk(xi) where pk(x) = e^fk(x)/Σe^fℓ(x). Penalizes incorrect predictions linearly.

name: huber loss
description: A robust regression loss criterion that is quadratic for small residuals |y-f(x)| ≤ δ and linear for large residuals, providing resistance to outliers.

name: gradient boosted models
description: A generalization of tree boosting that fits additive models using any differentiable loss function, providing robustness and accuracy for data mining.

name: partial dependence plot
description: A visualization showing the marginal effect of selected predictors on the predicted outcome, averaging over the other predictors to reveal predictor contributions.

name: steepest descent
description: An optimization method that chooses the increment vector as negative of the gradient times a scalar step size, moving in the direction of most rapid decrease.

name: gradient boosting
description: A boosting technique that uses the gradient of the loss function to fit trees at each iteration, generalizing boosting to arbitrary differentiable loss functions.

name: pseudo residuals
description: The negative gradient components in gradient boosting, also called generalized residuals, which serve as targets for fitting regression trees.

name: right-sized trees for boosting
description: The practice of using trees of fixed size J in boosting, where J controls the level of interaction effects that can be captured.

name: anova expansion
description: A decomposition of a function into main effects, second-order interactions, and higher-order interactions between variables.

name: main effect
description: The contribution of a single predictor variable to the target function, independent of interactions with other variables.

name: decision stump
description: A tree with a single split (J=2), producing boosted models with only main effects and no interactions.

name: early stopping
description: A regularization strategy where training is terminated before reaching the global minimum, monitoring validation error to prevent overfitting.

name: shrinkage in boosting
description: Scaling the contribution of each tree by a learning rate factor 0 < ν < 1 when added to the current approximation, controlling the learning rate of boosting.

name: learning rate in boosting
description: The parameter ν controlling how much each tree contributes to the boosted model, with smaller values requiring more iterations but often yielding better test error.

name: stochastic gradient boosting
description: A variant of gradient boosting that samples a fraction of training observations at each iteration before growing the next tree, improving performance and computational efficiency.

name: subsampling in boosting
description: Sampling a fraction of training data at each boosting iteration to reduce computation time and often improve accuracy.

name: relative importance of predictor variables
description: A measure quantifying the relevance of each input variable in boosted tree models, based on squared improvements in the splitting criterion.

name: partial dependence plots
description: Graphical visualization showing the marginal effect of a subset of predictor variables on the prediction function after accounting for the average effects of other variables.

name: weak learner
description: An algorithm for producing a two-class classifier with performance guaranteed to be significantly better than random guessing.

name: projection pursuit regression
description: A regression model that fits additive nonlinear functions of linear combinations (projections) of the inputs, where both the projection directions and ridge functions are estimated from data.

name: ridge function
description: A function in p-dimensional space that varies only in the direction defined by a vector, of the form g(ωᵀX).

name: single index model
description: A projection pursuit regression model with M=1, known in econometrics, slightly more general than linear regression.

name: gauss-newton search
description: A quasi-Newton optimization method that discards the part of the Hessian involving second derivatives, used for fitting projection pursuit models.

name: single hidden layer perceptron
description: A neural network with one hidden layer, also called a vanilla neural net or single hidden layer back-propagation network.
